column1:
According to the attached color map, what color will laminar flow be demonstrated that is moving at 40cm/s away from the probe?

A: blue
B: red
C: green
D: yellow
column2:
Answer: A. blue

Feedback:
The color on the bottom of the color bar indicates flow moving away from the transducer. The variance map allows for
evaluation of turbulent flow. If a green color appears, the flow is moving away from the probe and is turbulent. Flow velocities
greater than 0.45 m/s will alias causing display of yellow (turbulent) or red.

column1:
The profunda artery:

A: supplies blood to the prostate and pelvic organs in a male patient
B: courses posterior and medial to the femoral artery
C: courses posterior and lateral to the femoral artery
D: demonstrates low resistance flow compared to the femoral artery
column2:
Answer: C. courses posterior and lateral to the femoral artery

Feedback:
The profunda artery courses posterior and lateral to the femoral artery.

column1:
The Doppler tracing most likely came from which normal lower extremity artery?

A: Dorsalis pedis
B: Popliteal
C: Peroneal
D: none of the above
column2:
Answer: D. none of the above

Feedback:
The image demonstrates a low resistance, monophasic waveform which is most consistent with "organ flow". Flow in the legs
normally demonstrates a high resistance, triphasic waveform.

column1:
The external iliac artery:

A: begins at the level of the inguinal ligament
B: courses medially through adductor hiatus
C: courses along the medial side of the psoas muscle
D: supplies the pelvic organs
column2:
Answer: C. courses along the medial side of the psoas muscle

Feedback:
The external iliac artery travels laterally to the inguinal ligament and becomes the common femoral artery as it crosses under
the ligament. It also courses along the medial side of the psoas muscle to supply the lower extremities with blood

column1:
The posterior tibial artery courses along:

A: the medial aspect of the thigh.
B: the medial aspect of the calf.
C: the lateral aspect of the calf.
D: the posterior aspect of the knee
column2:
Answer: B. the medial aspect of the calf.

Feedback:
The posterior tibial artery courses along the medial aspect of the calf.

column1:
Vasoconstriction will cause ________________ , while vasodilation will cause ______________.

A: increased heart rate, increased blood pressure
B: increased flow volume, decreased flow volume
C: increased resistance to flow, decreased resistance to flow
D: increased flow rate, decreased flow rate
column2:
Answer: C. increased resistance to flow, decreased resistance to flow

Feedback:
Vasoconstriction in the arterioles will cause increased resistance to flow in the feeding vessels, while vasodilation will cause
reduced resistance.

column1:
Which of the following arteries is not commonly evaluated in an arterial assessment of the lower extremity?

A: internal iliac artery
B: superficial femoral artery
C: common femoral artery
D: anterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: A. internal iliac artery

Feedback:
The internal iliac artery supplies the pelvic organs and is much smaller in caliber than the external iliac artery that supplies the
leg.

column1:
Exercise causes ________________ which leads to _________________ in the lower extremities.

A: vasodilation, increased resistance
B: vasoconstriction, increased resistance
C: vasodilation, decreased resistance
D: vasoconstriction, decreased resistance
column2:
Answer: C. vasodilation, decreased resistance

Feedback:
Vasodilation occurs and resistance to flow drops to encourage more flow volume distally, as the muscles require more oxygen
with exercise. The resistance to flow decreases and pressure increases with exercise to increase the flow volume to the
functioning muscles. If enough blood does not reach the muscles, symptoms like claudication can occur.

column1:
Which of the following arteries does not usually have a pair of veins of the same name?

A: gastrocnemius artery
B: peroneal artery
C: ulnar artery
D: femoral artery
column2:
Answer: D. femoral artery

Feedback:
Femoral veins can be duplicated but this not a common finding and is considered a variant. The forearm and calf arteries have
paired veins of the same name.

column1:
What blood vessels are located at letter A?

A: 1 PTA and 2 PTV
B: 1 ATA and 2 ATV
C: 2 PTA and 1 PTV
D: 2 ATA and 1 ATV
column2:
Answer: B. 1 ATA and 2 ATV

Feedback:

column1:
Which of the following describes the recommended protocol for an accurate evaluation of the lower extremity arteries?

A: Flex the foot and rotate the ankle when performing Doppler evaluation of the distal posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries
B: Place the patient in the Trendelenburg position
C: Patients with hip pain can be placed in an oblique or prone position to obtain the Doppler samples from the popliteal artery
D: Rotate the leg internally and bend the knee slightly
column2:
Answer: C. Patients with hip pain can be placed in an oblique or prone position to obtain the Doppler samples from the popliteal artery

Feedback:
Patients should be placed in the supine position for a routine examination. Rotate the leg externally and bend the knee slightly
for Doppler evaluation. The foot and ankle should always be in the neutral position when performing a Doppler evaluation of
the distal PTA and DPA. Patients with hip pain can be placed in an oblique or prone position to obtain the Doppler samples from
the popliteal artery

column1:
Which arteries supply the forearm and hand with blood?

A: 2 Radial and 2 Ulnar Arteries
B: 1 Radial Artery and 1 Ulnar Artery
C: 1 Cephalic Artery and 1 Basilic Artery
D: 2 Radial and 1 Ulnar arteries
column2:
Answer: B. 1 Radial Artery and 1 Ulnar Artery

Feedback:

column1:
What are the direct branches of the popliteal artery?

A: posterior tibial artery and anterior tibial artery
B: Anterior tibial artery and tibioperoneal trunk
C: Posterior tibial artery and tibioperoneal trunk
D: Peroneal and posterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: B. Anterior tibial artery and tibioperoneal trunk

Feedback:
The ATA branches off the popliteal artery first, leaving the tibioperoneal trunk that then splits into the peroneal and PTA slightly
more distally in the calf.

column1:
The profunda femoral artery supplies blood to which of the following structures?

A: Quadriceps muscle
B: Bicep muscle
C: Pelvic organs
D: Psoas muscle
column2:
Answer: A. Quadriceps muscle

Feedback:
The profunda artery branches from the common femoral artery and mainly supplies blood to the muscles/structures of the
thigh and hip.

column1:
A normal penile brachial index is:

A: >0.8
B: >0.5
C: >0.75
D: >0.6
column2:
Answer: C. >0.75

Feedback:
The PBI is considered normal if it is greater than 0.75. Vasculogenic impotence is diagnosed when the PBI falls below 0.65. A PBI
between 0.65 and 0.75 is considered suspicious for vasculogenic impotence but further evaluation is necessary.

column1:
Which vessel wall layer is thicker in arteries than in veins?

A: vasa vasorum
B: tunica externa
C: tunica media
D: tunica intima
column2:
Answer: C. tunica media

Feedback:
The muscular layer is thicker in arteries to help maintain their shape and to handle the higher pressure blood flow within.

column1:
The normal brachial artery waveform should resemble the flow in the normal ______________artery

A: superior mesenteric artery
B: peroneal artery
C: vertebral artery
D: hepatic artery
column2:
Answer: B. peroneal artery

Feedback:
The normal brachial artery waveform will normally resemble the flow in the normal peroneal artery. Extremity flow will
demonstrate a high resistance, triphasic waveform.

column1:
Which artery courses anteriorly along the calf and laterally through the interosseous membrane of the leg?

A: anterior tibial artery
B: peroneal artery
C: distal superficial femoral artery
D: posterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: A. anterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The interosseous membrane connects the medial tibia and anterior fibula and separates the muscles of the calf. The ATA and
ATV course just anterior to the membrane.

column1:
What causes the 3rd phase of flow in a normal extremity arterial waveform?

A: cardiac systole
B: arterial wall compliance
C: relaxation of the extremity musculature
D: relaxation of the left ventricle
column2:
Answer: B. arterial wall compliance

Feedback:

column1:
The lateral plantar arch artery originates at the _____________________.

A: popliteal artery
B: posterior tibial artery
C: anterior tibial artery
D: peroneal artery
column2:
Answer: B. posterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The lateral plantar arch originates at the PTA.

column1:
What is the name of the tiny network of vessels that supply/drain blood to/from the vessel wall?

A: venules
B: capillaries
C: arterioles
D: vasa vasorum
column2:
Answer: D. vasa vasorum

Feedback:
The tiny network of vessels that supply/drain blood to/from the vessel wall is called the vasa vasorum. The tiny vessels wrap
around the larger vessel within the tunica externa. The arteries and veins penetrate the tunica media to deliver and drain blood
from the wall.

column1:
Where will you place the transducer to evaluate flow in the posterior tibial artery?

A: posterior to the lateral malleolus
B: posterior to the medial malleolus
C: anterior to the medial malleolus
D: on the foot, between the 1st and 2nd digit
column2:
Answer: B. posterior to the medial malleolus

Feedback:
To evaluate flow in the PTA, the transducer is placed posterior to the medial malleolus. The PTA courses between the medial
malleolus and the Achilles tendon.

column1:
The popliteal artery ___________ the popliteal vein.

A: courses anterior to
B: is larger than
C: is more tortuous than
D: courses posterior to
column2:
Answer: A. courses anterior to

Feedback:
The popliteal artery courses anterior to the popliteal vein.
When answering questions regarding anatomic relationships and considering US images, always start by considering the
transducer placement on the body. The popliteal vein is imaged by placing the transducer in the posterior popliteal fossa. The
anterior aspect of this image is the posterior aspect of the patient.

column1:
What blood vessels are located at letter B?

A: 2 peroneal arteries and 1 peroneal vein
B: 1 PTA and 2 PTV
C: 2 peroneal veins and 1 peroneal artery
D: 1 ATA and 2 ATV
column2:
Answer: C. 2 peroneal veins and 1 peroneal artery

Feedback:

column1:
Letter A indicates which of the following structures?

A: Vertebral artery
B: Thyrocervical trunk
C: Superficial temporal artery
D: Internal thoracic artery
column2:
Answer: D. Internal thoracic artery

Feedback:
A. Internal thoracic artery
B. Vertebral artery
C. Thyrocervical trunk
D. Right CCA

column1:
What two vessels are palpated to assess the pulse when peripheral arterial disease is suspected?

A: posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries
B: posterior tibial and peroneal arteries
C: dorsalis pedis and peroneal arteries
D: anterior tibial and peroneal arteries
column2:
Answer: A. posterior tibial and dorsalis pedis arteries

Feedback:
If peripheral arterial disease is suspected, the physician will palpate the PTA and DPA to assess the strength of the pulse. The
PTA is palpated between the posterior border of the medial malleolus and the Achilles tendon. The DPA is palpated at the dorsal
most prominence of the navicular bone. The peroneal artery is not usually able to be palpated due to its anatomic course.

column1:
Which of the following describes how to correctly measure the width of the iliac artery?

A: longitudinal view, outer wall to inner wall
B: longitudinal view, outer wall to outer wall
C: transverse view, outer wall to inner wall
D: transverse view, outer wall to outer wall
column2:
Answer: D. transverse view, outer wall to outer wall

Feedback:
When evaluating the diameter of the aorta and iliac arteries, AP Dimensions are obtained in longitudinal plane just like the
aorta, from outer wall to outer wall of the vessel. Width Dimensions are obtained in the transverse plane, from outer wall to
outer wall of the vessel. Many vascular labs repeat the AP dimension in the transverse plane to correlate with the preferred AP
dimension.

column1:
What effect will increased hematocrit levels have on an arterial duplex exam?

A: decreased blood flow velocities
B: decreased blood viscosity
C: hematocrit levels do not affect a Doppler evaluation
D: increased color flow display
column2:
Answer: A. decreased blood flow velocities

Feedback:
Increased hematocrit levels = Increased viscosity = Decreased flow velocities. Honey, syrup, and oil are examples of liquids with
elevated viscosity compared to blood/water. Consider a spoonful of water and a spoonful of honey. Which one takes longer to
flow off the spoon into a cup? The honey takes significantly longer to drain off the spoon than water. Higher viscosity = slower
flow

column1:
The inguinal ligament:

A: connects the greater tuberosity to the superior edge of the iliac crest
B: connects the greater and lesser tuberosities of the femur
C: connects the ischial tuberosity to the anterior pubic bone
D: connects the superior edge of the iliac crest with the anterior pubic bone
column2:
Answer: D. connects the superior edge of the iliac crest with the anterior pubic bone

Feedback:
The inguinal ligament connects the superior edge of the iliac crest to the anterior pubic bone.

column1:
Which of the following principles produces an equation that relates pressure gradient to flow and resistance within the circulatory system?

A: Poiseuille's Law
B: Bernoulli Principle
C: Doppler Principle
D: Raynaud's Phenomenon
column2:
Answer: A. Poiseuille's Law

Feedback:
Poiseuille's Law states the pressure gradient in a vessel is equal to the flow volume multiplied by the resistance. Bernoulli's
equation uses flow velocity to determine the pressure gradient at a specific location within the circulatory system.

column1:
A weak pulse on the posterior tibial artery will be described as ___________, while a bounding pulse in the anterior tibial artery will be described as _____________.

A: 0, 2+
B: 4+, 0
C: 1+, 4+
D: 2+, 6+
column2:
Answer: C. 1+, 4+

Feedback:
Pulses are described as 0 none, 1+ weak, 2+ good, 3+ strong, 4+ bounding

column1:
Which waveform demonstrates the lowest resistance?

A: D
B: A
C: C
D: B
column2:
Answer: A. D

Feedback:
A. monophasic, higher resistance
B. biphasic
C. Triphasic
D. monophasic, lower resistance

column1:
The _______________ artery terminates at the external calcaneal artery.

A: peroneal
B: anterior tibial
C: posterior tibial
D: medial gastrocnemius
column2:
Answer: A. peroneal

Feedback:
The peroneal artery terminates at the external calcaneal artery.

column1:
What blood vessels are demonstrated on the cross section of the penis?

A: cavernosal arteries
B: urethral arteries
C: helicine arteries
D: dorsal arteries
column2:
Answer: A. cavernosal arteries

Feedback:
The image demonstrates a cross section of the penis. The two corpus cavernosum and the corpus spongiosum are identified.
The cavernosal artery courses through the corpus cavernosum.
Penile Vasculature: Internal iliac arteries give rise to the internal pudendal arteries, bulbourethral artery, and penile arteries.
The penile artery branches into the dorsal and cavernosal arteries. The right and left cavernosal arteries enter the right and left
CC, respectively. The helicine arteries are multiple tiny branches that penetrate the tissue. Superficial dorsal vein and deep
dorsal vein provide the primary venous outflow routes

column1:
You are evaluating the subclavian artery and its branches. How can you differentiate the vertebral artery from the thyrocervical and costocervical trunks?

A: the vertebral artery demonstrates flow moving toward the transducer and the trunk arteries demonstrate flow moving away from the transducer
B: the vertebral artery demonstrates flow moving away from the transducer and the trunk arteries demonstrate flow toward the transducer
C: the trunk arteries have many branches and lower end diastolic flow velocities
D: the vertebral artery has many branches and lower end diastolic flow velocities
column2:
Answer: C. the trunk arteries have many branches and lower end diastolic flow velocities

Feedback:
Thyrocervical and Costocervical Trunk arteries can be differentiated from the vertebral arteries by their many branches and
lower end-diastolic flow velocities. Flow direction should not be used to identify these vessels because of the many variables in
evaluation technique and potential flow abnormalities (like a subclavian steal).

column1:
The inner most layer of an arterial wall is called ____________________, while the outermost layer of an arterial wall is called _______________________.

A: vasa vasorum, tunica adventitia
B: tunica intima, tunica adventitia
C: tunica intima, vasa vasorum
D: tunica adventitia, tunica intima
column2:
Answer: B. tunica intima, tunica adventitia

Feedback:
The inner most layer of an arterial wall is called tunica intima, while the outermost layer of an arterial wall is called tunica
adventitia.

column1:
Which of the waveforms demonstrates antegrade flow in diastole?

A: A, C, D
B: A, B, D
C: B only
D: C only
column2:
Answer: A. A, C, D

Feedback:
A. monophasic, high resistance
B. biphasic
C. Triphasic
D. monophasic, low resistance
Monophasic arterial flow indicates forward flow throughout the cardiac cycle. Biphasic arterial flow has two components of flow,
antegrade flow in systole and retrograde flow in diastole. Triphasic arterial flow has three components of flow, antegrade flow in
systole, retrograde flow in diastole, and a small segment of antegrade flow at the end of diastole.

column1:
A normal peak velocity in the cavernosa arteries after a papaverine injection would be _____________

A: at least 50 cm/s
B: > 30 cm/s
C: <40 cm/s
D: <20 cm/s
column2:
Answer: B. > 30 cm/s

Feedback:
A normal peak velocity in the cavernosa arteries after a papaverine injection would be >30cm/s. Reduced velocity indicates
vasculogenic impotence.

column1:
How do you test the capillary blush response on the upper extremity?

A: Apply a blood pressure cuff to the upper arm and inflate above the systolic pressure for 90 seconds then release the cuff to time the return of normal coloring in the arm.
B: Compress the radial and ulnar artery simultaneously for 60 seconds to create pallor in the hand and then release to time the return of normal coloring in the hand
C: Raise the arm above the head for 60 seconds to produce pallor and then place the arm in neutral position to time the return of normal coloring in the arm.
D: Press your finger into the fleshy part of the arm to create pallor, then release to time the return of normal skin coloring.
column2:
Answer: D. Press your finger into the fleshy part of the arm to create pallor, then release to time the return of normal skin coloring.

Feedback:
Press your finger into the fleshy part of the arm to create pallor, then release to time the return of normal skin coloring. Longer
times indicate the presence of arterial disease or obstruction.

column1:
Vasodilation:

A: usually occurs in response to cold stimuli
B: can cause a monophasic waveform to become triphasic
C: can cause a triphasic waveform to become biphasic
D: usually occurs with stress or anxiety
column2:
Answer: C. can cause a triphasic waveform to become biphasic

Feedback:
Vasodilation causes reduced resistance to flow to meet the tissue demands. Exercise will cause vasodilation and a triphasic
waveform can become biphasic with the reduced resistance to flow in the lower extremities. The resistance to flow decreases
and pressure increases with exercise to increase the flow volume to the functioning muscles. If enough blood does not reach
the muscles, symptoms like claudication can occur.

column1:
The distal femoral artery becomes the popliteal artery:

A: at the level of the medial quadricep tendon
B: in the popliteal fossa
C: at the end of the adductor canal
D: at the level of the heads of the gastrocnemius muscles
column2:
Answer: C. at the end of the adductor canal

Feedback:
The distal femoral artery becomes the popliteal artery at the end of the adductor canal (Hunter's canal).

column1:
Which of the following statements is true regarding the tunica intima?

A: An intimal dissection requires generation of a preliminary report and a stat review by a physician.
B: The intimal layer of the artery will become less prominent with age and more difficult to visualize sonographically.
C: The vasa vasorum forms within the intimal layer.
D: The tunica intima is responsible for holding the vessel shape and allowing for wall flexibility.
column2:
Answer: A. An intimal dissection requires generation of a preliminary report and a stat review by a physician.

Feedback:
An intimal dissection requires generation of a preliminary report and a stat review by a physician. It is considered a critical
finding. The vasa vasorum surrounds the outer wall of the vessel to supply the walls with blood. The tunica media is responsible
for holding the vessel shape and allowing for wall flexibility. The intimal layer of the artery will become more prominent with age
and more easily visualized sonographically.

column1:
If the systemic blood pressure rises but flow remains constant, what happens to the resistance in the vascular beds?

A: increases
B: unable to determine without knowing the flow velocity
C: decreases
D: no change
column2:
Answer: A. increases

Feedback:
If Flow Volume (Q) remains the same, if pressure (P) changes, resistance (R) must also change to keep the same flow volume.
Resistance and pressure are directly related. If pressure goes up, resistance must go up to maintain a consistent amount of flow
volume.
Simplified Mathematic Example: Flow Volume (Q) 6 = (P) 12 / (R) 2
Pressure goes up to 24, Resistance must increase to 4 to calculate the same flow volume of 6. (24 / 4 = 6)

column1:
What is the primary factor that determines the resistance characteristics of the arterial signal displayed by PW Doppler?

A: Part it is supplying
B: Branch number
C: Vessel course and location
D: Origin of blood
column2:
Answer: A. Part it is supplying

Feedback:
EX: Renal artery is low resistance for a normal kidney. Organs need constant flow throughout the cardiac cycle. The femoral
artery is high resistance for a normal leg. Extremities do not need constant flow throughout the cardiac cycle.

column1:
Which of the following is NOT normally a part of a test for vasculogenic impotence?

A: Obtain blood pressures in the arms and ankles
B: Measure the length of the cavernous arteries
C: Obtain Doppler samples of the cavernous arteries and veins in the penis
D: Obtain Doppler samples of the CFA, PTA and DPA
column2:
Answer: B. Measure the length of the cavernous arteries

Feedback:
When performing a penile Doppler exam, obtain Doppler samples of the CFA, cavernous arteries and veins in the penis. Doppler
sample will also be taken in the PTA and DPA while performing ankle pressures. The ABI should be evaluated to determine if
PAD can be causing the erectile dysfunction. The diameter of the cavernous arteries should be measured.

column1:
Which vessel(s) supplies the majority of the blood to the hand?

A: ulnar artery
B: radial artery
C: metatarsal arteries
D: digital veins
column2:
Answer: A. ulnar artery

Feedback:
The ulnar artery supplies the majority of the blood to the hand.

column1:
What Sonographic landmark is used to identify the distal end of the common iliac artery?

A: the inguinal ligament
B: head of the quadricep muscle
C: the bifurcation into the external and internal iliac artery
D: the saphenofemoral junction
column2:
Answer: C. the bifurcation into the external and internal iliac artery

Feedback:
The common iliac artery ends at the bifurcation into the external and internal iliac arteries. When the external iliac artery passes
beneath the inguinal ligament, it becomes the common femoral artery. The inguinal ligament is an anatomic landmark for the
external iliac artery, but it cannot normally be visualized when evaluating the extremity arteries.

column1:
Which statement is true regarding the internal thoracic artery?

A: The Allen test must be performed to assess breast perfusion prior to harvesting the internal mammary artery.
B: It is a branch of the thoracic aorta.
C: It can be used as a coronary bypass graft.
D: It can be a source of collateral flow for the vertebrobasilar system.
column2:
Answer: C. It can be used as a coronary bypass graft.

Feedback:
The internal thoracic artery (AKA internal mammary artery) originates from the subclavian artery. It can be used as a coronary
bypass graft in cases where the GSV is unavailable. The Allen test is used to evaluate the palmar arch when removing the radial
artery.
https://creativecommons.org/licenses/by/3.0/deed.en

column1:
Which calf vessel can be identified between the medial malleolus and the distal portion of the Achilles tendon?

A: posterior venous arch
B: anterior tibial artery
C: peroneal artery
D: posterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: D. posterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The PTA and PTVs can be identified between the medial malleolus and the distal portion of the Achilles tendon.

column1:
You receive an order for a pre-op arterial mapping to evaluate the internal mammary artery. What vessel should you evaluate to locate the origin of the internal mammary artery?

A: innominate artery
B: descending aorta
C: aortic arch
D: subclavian artery
column2:
Answer: D. subclavian artery

Feedback:
The internal mammary artery (AKA internal thoracic artery) originates from the subclavian artery.

column1:
Diastolic flow reversal is normal in which of the following vessels?

A: celiac artery
B: internal carotid artery
C: renal arteries
D: common iliac artery
column2:
Answer: D. common iliac artery

Feedback:
The lower extremities are normally a high resistance vascular bed. This leads to diastolic flow reversal and a triphasic or
biphasic waveform. Any low resistance vessel with a monophasic waveform has no flow reversal during diastole.

column1:
The internal iliac artery:

A: demonstrates lower resistance flow than the external iliac artery.
B: branches into the superficial and deep femoral arteries
C: supplies the bladder, prostate and testicles in a male patient.
D: courses lateral to the external iliac artery to reach the pelvis.
column2:
Answer: A. demonstrates lower resistance flow than the external iliac artery.

Feedback:
Flow in the internal iliac artery is less resistant that the external branch because it supplies the pelvic organs with blood. The
internal iliac artery courses medial to the external iliac artery to reach the pelvis. The gonadal arteries originate from the aorta,
not the internal iliac artery. The common femoral artery bifurcates into the deep and superficial femoral arteries.

column1:
Which of the following statements refers to the internal pudendal artery?

A: Branch of the external iliac artery
B: Most common location for stenosis causing vasculogenic impotence
C: Is the injection site for papaverine
D: Supplies the penis with blood through the penile artery and its branches.
column2:
Answer: D. Supplies the penis with blood through the penile artery and its branches.

Feedback:
The internal pudendal artery is a branch of the internal iliac artery and supplies the penis with blood through the penile artery
and its branches. Papaverine is injected into the tissues at the lateral side of the base of the penis.

column1:
Ohm's Law of electrical current is also used to describe:

A: venous capacitance
B: the appearance of flow vortices distal to a stenosis
C: arterial phasicity related to vessel recoil
D: arterial blood flow volumes
column2:
Answer: D. arterial blood flow volumes

Feedback:
Ohm's Law of electrical current is also used to describe arterial blood flow volumes. current = voltage / resistance which equates
to flow volume = pressure / resistance. Ohm's Law equates to Poiseuille's Law

column1:
Normal extremity arterial flow should demonstrate a _________________acceleration time.

A: blunted
B: prolonged
C: rapid
D: variable
column2:
Answer: C. rapid

Feedback:
Normal extremity arterial flow should demonstrate a rapid acceleration time.

column1:
Which of the following correctly describes a normal Doppler waveform of an artery from the lower extremity?

A: The triphasic waveform never demonstrates a retrograde flow component during the cardiac cycle.
B: The triphasic waveform will have two components of antegrade flow during the cardiac cycle.
C: There will be a low resistance, monophasic waveform with a small amount of flow reversal in diastole.
D: The triphasic waveform will have two components of normal retrograde flow during the cardiac cycle.
column2:
Answer: B. The triphasic waveform will have two components of antegrade flow during the cardiac cycle.

Feedback:
The resistance in the peripheral vascular beds is very high compared to the abdominal organs. Blood flows into the extremities
during systole with flow reversal during diastole. The resistance in the vascular beds is greater than the resistance in the feeding
vessels during mid/end diastole causing flow reversal to occur in the vessel. This produces a waveform with at least two
separate phases of flow (biphasic) If the pressure gradient between the vascular bed and the feeding vessel continues to
equalize, a small amount of flow may move toward the vascular bed at the very end of diastole. This produces a waveform with
three separate phases of flow (triphasic), 2 antegrade and 1 retrograde component. Biphasic and triphasic flow is generally
considered normal in the extremities.

column1:
What blood vessels are located at letter C?

A: 1 ATA and 2 ATV
B: 2 peroneal arteries and 1 peroneal vein
C: 2 peroneal veins and 1 peroneal artery
D: 1 PTA and 2 PTV
column2:
Answer: D. 1 PTA and 2 PTV

Feedback:

column1:
Which of the following lists the most common anatomic variation in aortic arch anatomy?

A: Two branches originate from the arch: a shared innominate/left CCA trunk and the left subclavian artery
B: The coronary arteries originate from the innominate artery or the right CCA
C: Pulmonary sling
D: Double aortic arch
column2:
Answer: A. Two branches originate from the arch: a shared innominate/left CCA trunk and the left subclavian artery

Feedback:
The most common anatomic variant of the arch branches is only two direct branches of the arch:
1. The innominate and left CCA originate as one short segment and then split
2. Left subclavian artery

column1:
Which of the following describes the normal response to a Doppler evaluation for erectile dysfunction?

A: Normally the velocity of the cavernosal arteries will not change post injection but the dorsal venous flow will become pulsatile.
B: Normally the PSV and EDV of the cavernosal arteries will increase post injection, but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged.
C: Normally the PSV and EDV of the arteries will increase post injection and dorsal venous flow will become pulsatile.
D: Normally the PSV and EDV of the cavernosal arteries will decrease post injection but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged.
column2:
Answer: B. Normally the PSV and EDV of the cavernosal arteries will increase post injection, but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged.

Feedback:
Normally the PSV and EDV of the cavernosal arteries will increase post injection, but dorsal venous flow will remain unchanged.
Abnormal inflow will cause the velocity in the cavernosal arteries to be less than 30cm/s. Increased venous outflow can also be
responsible for impotence.

column1:
The vessel wall layer that is composed of fibrous connective tissue is called:

A: tunica intima
B: vasa vasorum
C: tunica media
D: tunica externa
column2:
Answer: D. tunica externa

Feedback:
The external layer is composed of connective tissue and adheres to the surrounding structures. The tunica media refers to the
muscle layer of the vessel wall. The inner layer is the tunica intima which is composed of epithelial cells and covered by the
basement membrane.

column1:
Which of the following describes how to correctly measure the AP diameter of iliac artery ?

A: transverse view, outer wall to outer wall
B: transverse view, outer wall to inner wall
C: longitudinal view, outer wall to inner wall
D: longitudinal view, outer wall to outer wall
column2:
Answer: D. longitudinal view, outer wall to outer wall

Feedback:
When evaluating the diameter of the aorta and iliac arteries, AP Dimensions are obtained in longitudinal plane, from outer wall
to outer wall of the vessel. Width Dimensions are obtained in the transverse plane, from outer wall to outer wall of the vessel.

column1:
The pulsatility index (PI) in a normal aorta should be ______________ the PI in the normal popliteal artery.

A: lower than
B: very similar to
C: at least 3 times greater than
D: higher than
column2:
Answer: A. lower than

Feedback:
The pulsatility index (PI) in a normal aorta should be lower than the PI in the normal popliteal artery. The aorta flow is lower
resistance than the popliteal artery, therefore there is a greater amount of pulsatility in the popliteal artery. The PI increases
from the central arteries to the peripheral extremity arteries. A PI <5.0 in a lower extremity artery indicates proximal
obstruction.

column1:
A branch of which artery joins the terminal ulnar artery to form the superficial palmar arch?

A: Antecubital
B: Radial
C: Ulnar
D: Brachial
column2:
Answer: B. Radial

Feedback:
A branch of the radial artery joins the terminal ulnar artery to form the superficial palmar arch. A branch of the ulnar artery
joins the terminal radial artery to form the deep palmar arch.

column1:
The dorsalis pedis artery is a branch of:

A: the anterior tibial artery
B: the peroneal artery
C: the gastrocnemius artery
D: the posterior tibial artery
column2:
Answer: A. the anterior tibial artery

Feedback:
The DPA is a distal branch of the ATA.

column1:
Which waveform can be described as biphasic?

A: D
B: A
C: B
D: C
column2:
Answer: C. B

Feedback:
A. monophasic, high resistance
B. biphasic
C. Triphasic
D. monophasic, low resistance

column1:
The term for the most common anatomic variant of the aortic arch anatomy is the:

A: Bifurcating arch
B: Talbot arch
C: Bovine arch
D: Simpson arch
column2:
Answer: C. Bovine arch

Feedback:
A Bovine arch indicates that there are two branches originating from the aortic arch, instead of 3. The innominate artery and left
CCA origin are combined and the left subclavian artery is the second branch. Although "bovine" indicates a cow, this arch
formation is NOT related to the arch formation in cattle.

column1:
While performing an arterial Duplex exam, the patient complains that she is cold. What effect could this have on your exam?

A: monophasic flow may be demonstrated in normal arteries
B: decreased ankle pressures
C: increased pulsatility in the vessels evaluated
D: decreased ankle pressures and monophasic flow may be demonstrated in normal arteries
column2:
Answer: C. increased pulsatility in the vessels evaluated

Feedback:
Cold temperatures cause vasoconstriction in the medium to small arteries of the body. This will lead to increased flow
resistance and pulsatility of the flow.

column1:
Which vessels allow for the exchange of nutrients and waste products between blood and tissue?

A: segmental arteries
B: plantar and palmar arches
C: distal arteries
D: capillaries
column2:
Answer: D. capillaries

Feedback:
Capillaries are the smallest vessels in the circulatory system and they allow for the exchange of nutrients and waste products
between blood and tissue.

column1:
Which of the following normal arteries is not palpable on physical exam?

A: common femoral
B: peroneal
C: dorsalis pedis
D: posterior tibial
column2:
Answer: B. peroneal

Feedback:
Because the location in the calf, the peroneal artery is not normally palpable.

column1:
What is the preferred patient position for performing an upper extremity arterial duplex exam?

A: arm abducted 45 degrees and externally rotated
B: arm adducted 45 degrees and internally rotated
C: arm abducted 90 degrees and externally rotated
D: arm raised above head with patient in supine position
column2:
Answer: A. arm abducted 45 degrees and externally rotated

Feedback:
The preferred patient position for performing an upper extremity arterial duplex exam is to abduct the arm 45 degrees with
external rotation.

column1:
The subclavian artery becomes the axillary artery:

A: at the level of the first rib
B: at the level of the fourth rib
C: when it crosses anterior to the clavicle
D: when it crosses over the deltoid muscle
column2:
Answer: A. at the level of the first rib

Feedback:
The subclavian artery extends from the innominate artery to the axillary artery. It courses posterior to the anterior scalene
muscle and clavicle. It becomes the axillary artery at the level of the first rib.

column1:
Where do the plantar arteries originate?

A: from the posterior tibial artery posterior to the medial malleolus
B: from the peroneal artery just past the Achilles tendon attachment at the heel
C: from the anterior tibial artery posterior to the medial malleolus
D: from the anterior tibial artery anterior to the medial malleolus
column2:
Answer: A. from the posterior tibial artery posterior to the medial malleolus

Feedback:
The plantar arteries originate from the PTA posterior to the medial malleolus.

column1:
Which vessel courses posterior to the anterior scalene muscle?

A: aorta
B: subclavian artery
C: brachial artery
D: axillary artery
column2:
Answer: B. subclavian artery

Feedback:
The subclavian artery courses posterior to the anterior scalene muscle.

column1:
Which of the following can be used to describe a normal waveform obtained from a CW Doppler evaluation of the femoral artery?

A: low amplitude
B: delayed deceleration
C: rapid upstroke
D: no diastolic flow reversal
column2:
Answer: C. rapid upstroke

Feedback:
Normal waveforms are triphasic with rapid upslope, sharp peak, rapid downslope, a small peak below the baseline representing
diastolic flow reversal, followed by a small peak of flow above the baseline

column1:
Which of the following is an expected change in flow in the brachial artery that occurs when the patient clenches their fist during the Doppler exam?

A: increased diastolic flow
B: decreased resistive index
C: increased pulsatility index
D: triphasic to monophasic waveform change
column2:
Answer: C. increased pulsatility index

Feedback:
Clenching the fist causes an increase in distal resistance. The brachial artery will show increased pulsatility and resistance with
decreased diastolic flow. Isometric flexing of the foot/toes has the same effect on the Doppler evaluation of the lower extremity
arteries. Note the increased resistance to flow with the loss of the antegrade flow in diastole. Higher resistance waveforms
exhibit greater pulsatility index.

column1:
Put the following terms in order from largest to smallest in size. capillary, aorta, femoral artery, arteriole, tibial artery

A: capillary, arteriole, tibial artery, femoral artery, aorta
B: capillary, aorta, femoral artery, tibial artery, arteriole
C: aorta, capillary, femoral artery, tibial artery, arteriole
D: aorta, femoral artery, tibial artery, arteriole, capillary
column2:
Answer: D. aorta, femoral artery, tibial artery, arteriole, capillary

Feedback:
From largest to smallest: aorta, femoral artery, tibial artery, arteriole, capillary. Capillaries are the smallest blood vessels in the
body.

column1:
A pulsatility index of > 5.0 in the extremities indicates:

A: normal resistance
B: mildly decreased resistance
C: moderately decreased resistance
D: severely decreased resistance
column2:
Answer: A. normal resistance

Feedback:
The pulsatility index describes the resistance of the blood flow. The higher the PI, the greater the resistance. Normal PI in the
extremities is >5.0 and in most organs normal values are <1.5

column1:
Which of the following is not a branch of the subclavian artery?

A: costocervical trunk
B: posterior auricular artery
C: thyrocervical trunk
D: internal thoracic artery
column2:
Answer: B. posterior auricular artery

Feedback:
Branches from subclavian artery: Vertebral artery, Internal mammary artery (AKA internal thoracic artery), Thyrocervical trunk,
Costocervical trunk

column1:
What term refers to the capability of the vascular beds to alter the resistance to flow to maintain the levels of flow needed for normal function?

A: laminar flow
B: vasoconstriction
C: autoregulation
D: vasodilation
column2:
Answer: C. autoregulation

Feedback:
Autoregulation refers to the capability of the vascular beds to alter the resistance to flow to maintain the levels of flow needed
for normal function. Vasoconstriction and vasodilation are ways the vascular beds autoregulate the flow.

column1:
If the acceleration time in the common femoral artery is 110ms:

A: there is an AV malformation
B: the inflow vessels are normal
C: there is a distal stenosis
D: there is a proximal stenosis
column2:
Answer: B. the inflow vessels are normal

Feedback:
If the AT in the common femoral artery is >140ms, iliac disease is suspected. If the AT in both if the common femoral arteries is
>140ms, aortic disease is suspected. 110ms is a normal finding.
